title: practical english usage - Part 364
content:
vocabulary •  616   very and very much not He’s not very imaginative, (     He's little imaginative.) Note that little cannot be used in this way.

That meal wasn’ t very expensive. (= quite cheap.) It's not very warm -you’d better take a coat.

Not very expresses quite a low degree.

207.4.                                       ►           1 .

I:or very with superlatives {very first, very best, etc), ►                For the very same,     57 not I’m  (very) much  happier in my new job. (     .. .•very happier.. .) However, (very) much  is used before comparatives.

I came very quickly, (     ... very much  quickly?) not The situation is very serious, (    ... very much  serious.) n You’re very kind, ( < We   use very, not very much,  before adjectives and adverbs.

1  adjectives        and   adverbs:     very     kind,     very     quickly 616 very     and   very     much ►     87.

For used to + infinitive (e.g. / used to smoke), Note that used is pronounced /ju:st/ in this structure.

It took them a long time to grow used to getting up in the night.

Little by little, he became used to his new family.

You’ll soon get used to living in the country.

used to (. . .ing).

Get, become and sometimes grow (►  394) can also be used before 3 get    used      to   .. .ing,    etc I’ m  quite used to her little ways.

Used is an adjective in this structure, and can be modified by quite or very.

It was a long time before she was used to working with old people.

I’m  used to driving in London now, but it was hard at the beginning.

Be used to can be followed by -ing forms, but not infinitives (►   104.2).

the accent.

At the beginning I couldn't understand Londoners because I wasn't used to I’ ve lived in Central London for six years now, so I'm used to the noise.

much that it is no longer strange or new.

If a person is used to something, it is familiar; he or she has experienced it so 1  meaning 615 (be)  used      to Word    Problems      from    A to  Z  Section     31 vocabulary •  617   wait ►     389 and wake(n)         awake(n) ►     457 and wait,     hope,     expect          look     forward For the difference between wait for and expect, ►     457.

We're still awaiting instructions.

The  transitive verb await is formal, and is used mostly with abstract objects.

I waited (for) a very tong time for her answer.

The  time preposition for is often dropped after wait.

We'll have to wait for the photos to be ready. (     . . .wait that the photos That-clauses are not used, but an object + infinitive structure is possible.

Please wait for me here. (     Please wait m e here.) Before a direct object, wait for is used.

I'll wait to hear from you before I do anything.

Wait can be followed by an infinitive.

voyage,       journey,        travel          trip For very. . . indeed, ►  199.

There was a lot of snow on the road. (     There was very much  snow...) Very much  is not often used as a determiner in affirmative clauses (►   165.4).

Have you got very much  work to do?

She didn't have very much  money.

Very much  can also be a determiner before a noun.

I very much  like mountains. (     I like very mu ch mountains.) We   do not normally put very much  between a verb and its object.

We very much  enjoyed the party. (     We   very enjoyed-.. .) Very much  can be an adverbial.

4 very     much      (adverbial) very much  surprised...) We were very surprised when Dan passed his exam. (More common than ...

I'm very worried about Angela. (NOT   .. .very m uch worried. ..) For details,  ►    96.8.

But we  use very with some past participles that are used as adjectives.

Journey times will be very much  reduced by the new road. (     .. .very not She was very much  loved by her grandchildren. (     She was very loved.) Before past participles we  normally use very much.

3 past    participles:       very     much      loved,      very     worried 619 vocabulary  •         -ward(s) used as adjectives or adverbs.

ttɑekwɑrd(s), forward(s), northward(s), outward(s) and similar words can be 619 -ward(s) and         ►   eɜi want         will ►     280.
